format: warning -> engineering concern -> management decision -> launch approval -> physical failure -> disaster
chain_summary: Known SRM field-joint seal vulnerability and prior O-ring distress became a cold-weather engineering concern; the no-launch recommendation was challenged and reversed through management decision-making; incomplete escalation allowed launch in colder-than-experience conditions; the right SRB aft field joint leaked hot gas and destroyed the vehicle.

links.claim: The earliest warning signal was not the January 1986 weather forecast alone; it was the pre-existing evidence that the SRM joint design and seal behavior were not fully understood and had shown O-ring distress in testing and flight.
links.notes: The exact first warning depends on whether the boundary is design/test data or flight evidence. The strongest early flight-warning point is STS-2 erosion; the strongest recurring pattern begins after STS 41-B and becomes severe after 51-C and 51-B.

links.claim: Cold temperature converted the historical O-ring problem into an acute launch concern because previous distress was concentrated in lower-temperature flights and 51-L was outside prior O-ring experience.
links.notes: The evidence supports low temperature as a risk amplifier and decision trigger, not as the sole standalone cause.

links.claim: Thiokol engineering recommended no launch below 53 F O-ring temperature because the predicted launch condition was outside the known flight experience base.
links.notes: The recommendation is directly documented in the teleconference chronology and hearing testimony.

links.claim: NASA challenged the no-launch recommendation; Thiokol entered an internal caucus; engineer objections persisted; a smaller management review reversed the recommendation to launch.
links.notes: The contradiction is preserved: managers described the data as inconclusive and relied on secondary seal rationale, while engineers continued to object.

links.claim: The launch approval was made without senior decision makers having the complete O-ring history, initial no-launch recommendation, continuing engineer opposition, launch-constraint history, and safety-program participation.
links.notes: This is the strongest organizational link: information existed, but the decision structure did not force it to the people responsible for launch readiness.

links.claim: Launch proceeded in record-cold Shuttle conditions despite the acute O-ring concern and outside-experience-base warning.
links.notes: The artifact does not infer a single individual cause; it traces the decision pathway documented across teleconference and management records.

links.claim: At ignition, joint dynamics and low-temperature O-ring behavior likely prevented timely sealing in the right SRB aft field joint, allowing blow-by and erosion.
links.notes: The low-temperature O-ring actuation path is strongly supported, but the precise micro-mechanical combination among putty behavior, squeeze, ice, and dimensions remains partially unresolved.

links.claim: The leak developed into a flame plume, impinged on the External Tank and attachment structure, breached the hydrogen tank, and led to structural breakup.
links.notes: This is the highest-confidence physical sequence because it is backed by film, telemetry, and recovered-wreckage analysis.

links.claim: The vehicle broke up about 73 seconds after launch after External Tank structural failure and aerodynamic loads destroyed the Orbiter.
links.notes: The crew-loss fact is documented in the accident chapter; crew-survival timing is outside this causal-traceback scope.
